wiki_text: Jackals are medium-sized omnivorous mammals of the genus Canis, which also includes wolves, coyotes and the domestic dog.
viki_text: Jackal The Jackal looks like a simple domestic dog, but it is a skilled predator, has sharp and curved canines that allow him to eat small prey; it has long legs to run fast, commonly his coat is yellowish-brown with a black strip on his back. The scientific name of the jackal is "Canis adustus", belonging to the genus of "Canis" and to the class of mammals.

wiki_text: Jackals may occasionally assemble in small packs, for example, to scavenge a carcass, but they normally hunt either alone or in pairs. Etymology
viki_text: The pack is formed by a pair of jackals that commands, and other jackals who have joined the herd to capture preys more easily.

wiki_text: The intermediate size and shape of the Ethiopian wolf has at times led it to be regarded as a jackal, thus it has also been called the "red jackal" or the "Simien jackal". Species Interbreeding with dogs
viki_text: Evolution starts from the black-backed jackal, which evolves into the lateral striped jackal; after then comes African the wild dog, after him comes the Dhole, after the Dhole comes the Ethiopian wolf; his face looks very much like the wolf.

wiki_text: While the word "jackal" has historically been used for many small canids, in modern use it most commonly refers to three species: the closely related black-backed jackal and side-striped jackal of sub-Saharan Africa, and the golden jackal of south-central Eurasia, which is more closely related to other members of the genus Canis.
viki_text: There are two other types of jackal: the "canis aureus": the golden jackal, and the "Canis mesomelas": the black-backed jackal The lateral striped jackal, the most common one, lives in Central and Southern Africa.

wiki_text: ;Surface area: A substance that has a larger surface area will evaporate faster, as there are more surface molecules per unit of volume that are potentially able to escape. ;Temperature of the substance: the higher the temperature of the substance the greater the kinetic energy of the molecules at its surface and therefore the faster the rate of their evaporation. In the US, the National Weather Service measures the actual rate of evaporation from a standardized "pan" open water surface outdoors, at various locations nationwide.
viki_text: The rate of evaporation are affected by the liquid's exposed surface area (faster when increased), the humidity of surroundings (slower when increased), the presence of wind (faster when increased) and the temperature (faster when increased).

wiki_text: As the faster-moving molecules escape, the remaining molecules have lower average kinetic energy, and the temperature of the liquid decreases. This phenomenon is also called evaporative cooling.
viki_text: Liquid with high boiling points (those that boil at very high temperatures) tend to evaporate more slowly than those with lower boiling temperatures.

wiki_text: Evaporation of water occurs when the surface of the liquid is exposed, allowing molecules to escape and form water vapor; this vapor can then rise up and form clouds.
viki_text: The water evaporates into water vapor, the gas phase of water.

wiki_text: However, these liquids are evaporating.
viki_text: Different liquids evaporate at different temperatures.

wiki_text: If evaporation takes place in an enclosed area, the escaping molecules accumulate as a vapor above the liquid.
viki_text: When water is evaporating, it transforms into a gas called water vapor.

wiki_text: Three key parts to evaporation are heat, atmospheric pressure (determines the percent humidity), and air movement.
viki_text: There are many factors that affect the evaporation rate.

wiki_text: When a molecule near the surface absorbs enough energy to overcome the vapor pressure, it will escape and enter the surrounding air as a gas. When evaporation occurs, the energy removed from the vaporized liquid will reduce the temperature of the liquid, resulting in evaporative cooling.
viki_text: Evaporation is when a liquid becomes a gas.

wiki_text: With sufficient energy, the liquid will turn into vapor.
viki_text: The water vapor mixes with the air.

wiki_text: It was the first time the Greeks had beaten the Persians, proving that the Persians were not invincible, and that resistance, rather than subjugation, was possible.
viki_text: The Greeks realized with this victory that Persia could be defeated.

wiki_text: The Battle of Marathon () took place in 490 BC during the first Persian invasion of Greece.
viki_text: The Battle of Marathon was a battle in 490 BC between the Athenian army and the forces of the Persian Empire.

wiki_text: The Greek army decisively defeated the more numerous Persians, marking a turning point in the Greco-Persian Wars.
viki_text: The Greek army decisively defeated the more numerous Persians.

wiki_text: The battle was a defining moment for the young Athenian democracy, showing what might be achieved through unity and self-belief; indeed, the battle effectively marks the start of a "golden age" for Athens. This was also applicable to Greece as a whole; "their victory endowed the Greeks with a faith in their destiny that was to endure for three centuries, during which western culture was born".
viki_text: The victory marked the beginning of the Classical Age in Greece.

wiki_text: The battle was the culmination of the first attempt by Persia, under King Darius I, to subjugate Greece.
viki_text: It was the culmination of the first Persian invasion of Greece under Darius I.

wiki_text: The distance eventually became fixed at 26 miles 385 yards, or 42.195 km, though for the first years it was variable, being around -the approximate distance from Marathon to Athens.
viki_text: The exact distance from Marathon to Athens then became the name of a challenge, the marathon, which was to run that distance without collapsing as Pheidippides did.

wiki_text: The most famous legend associated with Marathon is that of the runner Pheidippides (or Philippides) bringing news to Athens of the battle, which is described below. Pheidippides' run to Sparta to bring aid has other legends associated with it.
viki_text: The word "marathon" originates from this battle - in legend, a warrior called Pheidippides ran from Marathon to Athens to inform the people of Athens of the Greek victory.

wiki_text: Languages Norwegian and Sámi are the two official languages of Norway. The North Germanic Norwegian language has two official written forms, Bokmål and Nynorsk.
viki_text: The main language of Norway is Norwegian (which has 2 written forms).

wiki_text: Most Norwegians are registered at baptism as members of the Church of Norway, which has been Norway's state church since its establishment.
viki_text: The majority of Norwegians are Protestant Christian.

wiki_text: Norway has obtained one of the highest standards of living in the world in part by having a large amount of natural resources compared to the size of the population.
viki_text: Norway is one of the most developed countries in the world.

wiki_text: Norwegians are an ethnic North Germanic people. Since the late 20th century, Norway has attracted immigrants from southern and central Europe, the Mideast, Africa, Asia and beyond.
viki_text: Norways' population is mostly ethnic Norwegians and people from the other Scandinavian countries.

wiki_text: Popular tourist destinations in Norway include Oslo, Ålesund, Bergen, Stavanger, Trondheim and Tromsø. Much of the nature of Norway remains unspoiled, and thus attracts numerous hikers and skiers.
viki_text: The most visited tourist sites in Norway are Holmenkollen Ski Jump and Bryggen which is a World Heritage Site.

wiki_text: Several Uralic Sámi languages are spoken and written throughout the country, especially in the north, by some members of the Sámi people.
viki_text: A variety of Sami languages are spoken by the Sami people especially in the north of the country.

wiki_text: The main attractions of Norway are the varied landscapes that extend across the Arctic Circle. It is famous for its fjord-indented coastline and its mountains, ski resorts, lakes and woods.
viki_text: Norway is a very expensive country but many tourists visit every year to see the famous fjords, the unspoiled nature and the attractive towns around the country.

wiki_text: According to the 2001 Census, 21 per cent of the population aged 3+ were able to speak Welsh. This suggests that there was a decrease in the number of Welsh speakers in Wales from 2001 to 2011 - from about 582,000 to 562,000 respectively.

wiki_text: At the National Eisteddfod of Wales 2009, a further announcement was made by the Welsh Language Board that the mobile phone company Samsung was to work with the network provider Orange to provide the first mobile phone in the Welsh language, with the interface and the T9 dictionary on the Samsung S5600 available in the Welsh language. wiki_text: The Welsh Language (Wales) Measure 2011 gave the Welsh language official status in Wales, making it the only language that is de jure official in any part of the United Kingdom, with English being de facto official.

wiki_text: "Head of government" is often differentiated from "head of state" , as they may be separate positions, individuals, or roles depending on the country.
viki_text: The head of government may not be confused with the head of state, which is "the leader of the country".

wiki_text: A common title for many heads of government is prime minister.
viki_text: In most countries the head of government is called a premier, first minister or prime minister (PM).

wiki_text: In parliamentary systems, including constitutional monarchies, the head of government is the de facto political leader of the government, and is answerable to one chamber or the entire legislature.
viki_text: In most monarchies, the head of government has more or far more influence in politics than the head of state.

wiki_text: In some models the head of state and head of government are one and the same.
viki_text: In some countries, the function of head of state and head of government are combined into one person.

wiki_text: The head of government is either the highest or second highest official in the executive branch of a sovereign state, a federated state, or a self-governing colony, who often presides over a cabinet, a group of ministers or secretaries who lead executive departments.
viki_text: The head of government is a person, who is the highest or second-highest official of a country or area. The head of government presides over the cabinet.

wiki_text: Early versions In November 1988, a new development team within Microsoft (which included former Digital Equipment Corporation developers Dave Cutler and Mark Lucovsky) began work on a revamped version of IBM and Microsoft's OS 2".
viki_text: As the hardware was evolving, IBM and Microsoft had started in 1985 to work together on a new operating system called OS 2 had been released already and a new version was being developed.

wiki_text: Following its approval by Microsoft's staff, development continued on what was now Windows NT, the first 32-bit version of Windows.
viki_text: That became Windows NT whose first version, released in 1993, immediately bore the number 3.1.

wiki_text: In August 1993, Windows for Workgroups, a special version with integrated peer-to-peer networking features and a version number of 3.11, was released. It was sold along with Windows 3.1. Support for Windows 3.1 ended on December 31, 2001.
viki_text: Until Windows 3.1 (1992) and Windows for Workgroup 3.11 (which brought some networking features in 1993), one had to buy and install DOS and then Windows.

wiki_text: In May 1999, Microsoft released Windows 98 Second Edition, an updated version of Windows 98. Windows 98 SE added Internet Explorer 5.0 and Windows Media Player 6.2 amongst other upgrades. Mainstream support for Windows 98 ended on June 30, 2002, and extended support for Windows 98 ended on July 11, 2006. On September 14, 2000, Microsoft released Windows Me (Millennium Edition), the last DOS-based version of Windows.
viki_text: Further evolutions of this branch included Windows 98 (1998) and Windows ME (Millenium Edition, in September 2000).

wiki_text: The next major consumer-oriented release of Windows, Windows 95, was released on August 24, 1995.
viki_text: Starting with Windows 95 (which came out in 1995), both layers were combined into one product.

wiki_text: Windows 3.0 is the first Microsoft Windows version to achieve broad commercial success, selling 2 million copies in the first six months. Windows 3.1, made generally available on March 1, 1992, featured a facelift.
viki_text: Windows 3.0 (1990) was the first popular version of Windows, with 7 million copies sold in the first year (mostly because Windows was then sold together with DOS when people bought a computer).

wiki_text: Groups of kangaroos are called mobs, which usually have 10 or more kangaroos in them.

wiki_text: Kangaroos are shy and retiring by nature, and in normal circumstances present no threat to humans.
viki_text: Besides humans and wild dogs called dingoes, kangaroos face few natural predators.

wiki_text: Kangaroos have a unique three-stage walk where they plant their front legs and tail first, then push off their tail, followed lastly by the back legs.
viki_text: To move at slow speeds, a kangaroo will use its tail to form a tripod with its two forelimbs.

wiki_text: Other suggested causes for erratic and dangerous kangaroo behaviour include extreme thirst and hunger.
viki_text: Heat, drought, and hunger due to loss of habitat are the biggest dangers kangaroos face.

wiki_text: Red kangaroos are commonly mistaken as the most abundant species of kangaroo, but eastern greys actually have a larger population.
viki_text: There are four species: the red kangaroo, the eastern grey kangaroo, the western grey kangaroo, and the antilopine kangaroo.

wiki_text: The comfortable hopping speed for a red kangaroo is about , but speeds of up to can be attained over short distances, while it can sustain a speed of for nearly .
viki_text: They stand over 6 feet (2 meters) tall. Thanks to their large feet, kangaroos can leap some 30 feet in a single bound, and travel more than 30 mph (48 kmh).

wiki_text: In an oratorio the choir often plays a central role, and there is generally little or no interaction between the characters, and no props or elaborate costumes. A particularly important difference is in the typical subject matter of the text. Opera tends to deal with history and mythology, including age-old devices of romance, deception, and murder, whereas the plot of an oratorio often deals with sacred topics, making it appropriate for performance in the church. Protestant composers took their stories from the Bible, while Catholic composers looked to the lives of saints, as well as to Biblical topics.
viki_text: Oratorios are usually, but not always, based on a Biblical subject.

wiki_text: Like most operas, an oratorio includes the use of a choir, soloists, an instrumental ensemble, various distinguishable characters, and arias. However, opera is musical theatre, while oratorio is strictly a concert piece - though oratorios are sometimes staged as operas, and operas are sometimes presented in concert form.
viki_text: An oratorio is a musical work for orchestra, vocal soloists, and choir.

wiki_text: *In the phonetic definition, a vowel is a sound, such as the English "ah" or "oh" , produced with an open vocal tract; it is median (the air escapes along the middle of the tongue), oral (at least some of the airflow must escape through the mouth), frictionless and continuant. There is no significant build-up of air pressure at any point above the glottis.
viki_text: A vowel sound comes from the lungs, through the vocal cords, and is not blocked, so there is no friction.

wiki_text: Moreover, a vowel might be represented by a letter usually reserved for consonants, or a combination of letters, particularly where one letter represents several sounds at once, or vice versa; examples from English include igh in "thigh" and x in "x-ray".
viki_text: The letter Y can be a vowel (as in the word "cry" or "candy"), or it can be a consonant (as in "yellow").

wiki_text: This has led to numerous inconsistencies in the spelling of English vowel sounds and the pronunciation of English vowel letters (and to the mispronunciation of foreign words and names by speakers of English).
viki_text: This important fact helps to explain why pronunciation can be difficult for both native speakers and learners of English.

wiki_text: Marie Taglioni, Comtesse de Voisins (23 April 1804 - 22 April 1884) was a Swedish ballet dancer of the Romantic ballet era, a central figure in the history of European dance.
viki_text: Marie Taglioni (23 April 1804 - 24 April 1884) was the first great romantic ballerina.

wiki_text: She is credited with (though not confirmed) being the first ballerina to truly dance en pointe.
viki_text: She was also the first ballerina to use pointe work in the service of art rather than acrobatics.

wiki_text: Taglioni rose to fame as a danseuse at the Paris Opéra when her father created the ballet La Sylphide (1832) for her.
viki_text: Taglioni became famous when her father created La Sylphide for her Paris debut.

wiki_text: Taglioni was born in Stockholm, Sweden, to Italian choreographer Filippo Taglioni and Swedish ballet dancer Sophie Karsten, maternal granddaughter of the Swedish opera singer Christoffer Christian Karsten and of the Polish opera singer and actress Sophie Stebnowska. Her brother, Paul (1808-1884), was also a dancer and an influential choreographer; they performed together early in their careers. Marriage
viki_text: She was born in Sweden to choreographer Filippo Taglioni, and Swedish ballerina Sophie Karsten.

wiki_text: A protist () is any eukaryotic organism (one with cells containing a nucleus) that is not an animal, plant, or fungus. viki_text: Protist Eukaryots are a large group of living beings (with the two others being bacteria and archae), recognisable by their cells with nucleus.

wiki_text: Brown recluse spiders are usually between , but may grow larger. While typically light to medium brown, they range in color from whitish to dark brown or blackish gray.
viki_text: The brown recluse spider can be light brown, dark brown, or even gray.

wiki_text: For definitive identification, it is imperative to examine the eyes. While most spiders have eight eyes, recluse spiders have six eyes arranged in pairs (dyads) with one medial pair and two lateral pairs.
viki_text: They have six eyes, which is an unusual number of eyes for a spider. Male and female brown recluses can be identified once the spider has molted several times.

wiki_text: The brown recluse, Loxosceles reclusa, Sicariidae (formerly placed in a family "Loxoscelidae") is a recluse spider with a necrotic venom.
viki_text: The brown recluse has six eyes (three pairs), unlike most spiders. The scientific name of the spider Loxosceles reclusa comes from words that mean "slanted leg recluse".

wiki_text: The spider usually bites only when pressed against the skin, such as when tangled within clothes, shoes, towels, bedding, inside work gloves, etc.
viki_text: Brown recluses will normally only bite a person if the person is touching the spider or if the spider is stuck between the person and something else.

wiki_text: These spiders usually have markings on the dorsal side of their cephalothorax, with a black line coming from it that looks like a violin with the neck of the violin pointing to the rear of the spider, resulting in the nicknames fiddleback spider, brown fiddler, or violin spider. wiki_text: "Negative theology is a complement to, not the enemy of, positive theology". Atheistic philosophies Axiological, or constructive, atheism rejects the existence of gods in favor of a "higher absolute", such as humanity.
viki_text: The opposite of atheism is theism, the belief that there is one or more gods.

wiki_text: Atheism and negative theology Apophatic theology is often assessed as being a version of atheism or agnosticism, since it cannot say truly that God exists. "The comparison is crude, however, for conventional atheism treats the existence of God as a predicate that can be denied ("God is nonexistent"), whereas negative theology denies that God has predicates".
viki_text: Agnosticism is a related belief, that the existence of God is unproven or cannot be proven, but which also doesn't say for certain that there is no God.

wiki_text: Less broadly, atheism is a rejection of the belief that any deities exist.
viki_text: Atheism, also known as irreligion, is the lack of belief in God or gods.

wiki_text: By this method, in North America, summer is the period from the summer solstice (usually 20 or 21 June in the Northern Hemisphere) to the autumn equinox.

wiki_text: In southern and southeast Asia, where the monsoon occurs, summer is more generally defined as lasting from March, April, May and June, the warmest time of the year, ending with the onset of the monsoon rains. Because the temperature lag is shorter in the oceanic temperate southern hemisphere, most countries in this region use the meteorological definition with summer starting on 1 December and ending on the last day of February.
viki_text: December, January, and February are generally considered the months of summer in the Southern Hemisphere.

wiki_text: The meteorological convention is to define summer as comprising the months of June, July, and August in the northern hemisphere and the months of December, January, and February in the southern hemisphere.
viki_text: June, July, and August are generally considered the months of summer in the Northern Hemisphere.

wiki_text: The French National Day is the anniversary of Storming of the Bastille on 14 July 1789, a turning point of the French Revolution, as well as the Fête de la Fédération which celebrated the unity of the French people on 14 July 1790. Celebrations are held throughout France.

wiki_text: "The Star-Spangled Banner" was recognized for official use by the United States Navy in 1889, and by U.S. President Woodrow Wilson in 1916, and was made the national anthem by a congressional resolution on March 3, 1931 (46 Stat. 1508, codified at ), which was signed by President Herbert Hoover. wiki_text: Colombia ( , ; ), officially the Republic of Colombia (), is a country largely situated in the north of South America, with land, and territories in North America.

wiki_text: Colombia is bounded on the north by the Caribbean Sea, the northwest by Panama, the south by both Ecuador and Peru, the east by Venezuela, the southeast by Brazil and the west by the Pacific.

wiki_text: Islam (; ) is an Abrahamic, monotheistic religion teaching that there is only one God (Allah), and that Muhammad is a messenger of God.
viki_text: Islam Like two other religions today, Judaism and Christianity, Islam is thought to be an Abrahamic religion, because the three religions are believed to have been started by Abraham. In all three religions, Abraham is one of God's earliest messengers.

wiki_text: It is the world's second-largest religion with over 1.9 billion followers or 24.4% of the world's population, commonly known as Muslims. Muslims make up a majority of the population in 50 countries. Islam teaches that God is merciful, all-powerful, and unique, and has guided mankind through prophets, revealed scriptures and natural signs.
viki_text: Islam is currently the second largest religion in the world with just above 1 billion followers.

wiki_text: Religiosity appears to be deepening worldwide.
viki_text: It is also the fastest growing religion in the world.

wiki_text: Dorian is one of only two Category 5 hurricanes to make landfall on the Abaco Islands, the other having occurred in 1932, and is the only such storm on record to have impacted Grand Bahama. viki_text: Hurricane Dorian was an extremely powerful and devastating Category 5 Atlantic hurricane, which became the most intense tropical cyclone on record to strike the Bahamas, and is also regarded as the worst natural disaster in the country's recorded history.

wiki_text: About 55% of blood is blood plasma, a fluid that is the blood's liquid medium, which by itself is straw-yellow in color.

wiki_text: Albumin is the main protein in plasma, and it functions to regulate the colloidal osmotic pressure of blood. The blood cells are mainly red blood cells (also called RBCs or erythrocytes), white blood cells (also called WBCs or leukocytes) and platelets (also called thrombocytes).
viki_text: These proteins are albumin, antibodies (also called immunoglobulins), clotting factors, and protein hormones.

wiki_text: Plasma, which constitutes 55% of blood fluid, is mostly water (92% by volume), and contains proteins, glucose, mineral ions, hormones, carbon dioxide (plasma being the main medium for excretory product transportation), and blood cells themselves.
viki_text: Plasma is made up of nutrients, electrolytes (salts), gases, non-protein hormones, waste, lipids, and proteins.

wiki_text: Headache is the symptom of pain anywhere in the region of the head or neck.
viki_text: A headache is a painful sensation anywhere in your head.

wiki_text: Migraines are often unilateral, pulsing headaches accompanied by nausea or vomiting.
viki_text: Really bad headaches are sometimes called migraines.

wiki_text: Treatment of a headache depends on the underlying cause, but commonly involves pain medication.
viki_text: There are many reasons for headaches, and sometimes they go away by themselves, or you might need to go to a doctor.

wiki_text: Cricket, however, is the most popular game across the country.
viki_text: The most popular sport in Pakistan is Cricket, Almost all districts and neighborhoods in Pakistan have a cricket team and people start playing from a young age.

wiki_text: The country has had an array of success in the sport over the years, and has the distinct achievement of having won each of the major ICC international cricket tournaments: ICC Cricket World Cup, ICC World Twenty20, and ICC Champions Trophy; as well as the ICC Test Championship. The cricket team (known as Shaheen) won the Cricket World Cup held in 1992; it was runner-up once, in 1999. Pakistan was runner-up in the inaugural World Twenty20 in South Africa and won the World Twenty20 in England in 2009.
viki_text: Pakistan has won international cricket events, which include the 1992 Cricket World Cup, the 2009 ICC World Twenty20 and the 2017 ICC Champions Trophy besides finishing as runner-up in the 1999 Cricket World Cup and the 2007 ICC World Twenty20.

wiki_text: Etymology "Gold" is cognate with similar words in many Germanic languages, deriving via Proto-Germanic ("to shine, to gleam; to be yellow or green").
viki_text: Gold gets its English name from the Germanic word gulþa (meaning gold).

wiki_text: Gold does not react with oxygen at any temperature and, up to 100 °C, is resistant to attack from ozone.
viki_text: Gold does not "react" with many other chemicals.

wiki_text: In its purest form, it is a bright, slightly reddish yellow, dense, soft, malleable, and ductile metal. Chemically, gold is a transition metal and a group 11 element.
viki_text: Gold is a yellow precious metal that is valued for its beauty and shine.

wiki_text: The jaguar (Panthera onca) is a large felid species and the only extant member of the genus Panthera native to the Americas.
viki_text: Jaguar The jaguar is a mammal, has 4 legs, is a very great predator, lives in the jungle, its scientific name is panther onca, it has a coat with dark brown black-brown spots, green eyes, a long, bushy tail and a face like a cat, he often climbs trees, a very large paws, the jaguar is very fast.

wiki_text: In some developing and developed nations, the domestic pig is usually raised outdoors in yards or fields.
viki_text: Where there is a demand for their meat, pigs are raised in large "factory farms" or on a small scale, even for a farm family's own use.

wiki_text: Most domestic pigs have rather a bristled sparse hair covering on their skin, although woolly-coated breeds such as the Mangalitsa exist.
viki_text: In fact, there are many breeds of pigs, and many have light color, e. g. white, or pinkish, but some are brown or black, or even spotted.

wiki_text: Other food products made from pigs include pork sausage (which includes casings that are made from the intestines), bacon, gammon, ham and pork rinds.
viki_text: These includes fresh pork and also processed and cured forms such as sausage, ham, and bacon.

wiki_text: In 1984, the National Wildlife Federation listed hunting, power-line electrocution, and collisions in flight as the leading causes of eagle deaths. Bald eagles have also been killed by oil, lead, and mercury pollution, and by human and predator intrusion at nests.

wiki_text: Cyprus (; ; ), officially the Republic of Cyprus (, ; , ), is an island country in the Eastern Mediterranean and the third largest and third most populous island in the Mediterranean, located south of Turkey, west of Syria and Lebanon, northwest of Israel and Palestine, north of Egypt, and southeast of Greece.
viki_text: Cyprus Map showing the location of Cyprus in Europe and western Asia Cyprus is an island nation in the eastern Mediterranean Sea.

wiki_text: Cyprus has two official languages, Greek and Turkish.
viki_text: Greek and Turkish are both official languages.

wiki_text: Cyprus is the third largest island in the Mediterranean Sea, after the Italian islands of Sicily and Sardinia or Western Asia and the Middle East.
viki_text: It is a large island, the 3rd biggest in the Mediterranean after Sicily and Sardinia (both islands of Italy).

wiki_text: History Prehistoric and Ancient Cyprus The earliest confirmed site of human activity on Cyprus is Aetokremnos, situated on the south coast, indicating that hunter-gatherers were active on the island from around 10,000 BC, with settled village communities dating from 8200 BC.
viki_text: People have lived in Cyprus since before 10,000 BCE.

wiki_text: The highest point on Cyprus is Mount Olympus at , located in the centre of the Troodos range.
viki_text: The highest mountain in Cyprus is Mount Olympus at 1,952 metres.

wiki_text: The international community considers the northern part of the island to be territory of the Republic of Cyprus occupied by Turkish forces.
viki_text: The northern part of Cyprus is known as the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus and is only recognized by Turkey.

wiki_text: The Perso-Arabic script form of this language underwent a standardisation process and further Persianization in the late Mughal period (18th century) and came to be known as Urdu, a name derived from the Turkic word ordu (army) or orda and is said to have arisen as the "language of the camp", or "Zaban-i-Ordu" (or natively "Lashkari Zaban"). As a literary language, Urdu took shape in courtly, elite settings. The name Urdu was first used by the poet Ghulam Hamadani Mushafi around 1780. Hindustani in Persian script was used by Muslims and Hindus, but was current chiefly in Muslim-influenced society.
viki_text: Urdu is a language written in the Perso-Arabic Script, and has many letters common with Arabic and Persian.

wiki_text: Urdu has been one of the premier languages of poetry in South Asia for two centuries, and has developed a rich tradition in a variety of poetic genres.
viki_text: Urdu is rich in both prose and poetry.

wiki_text: Urdu is therefore spoken and understood by the vast majority in some form or another, including a majority of urban dwellers in such cities as Karachi, Lahore, Okara District, Sialkot, Rawalpindi, Islamabad, Multan, Faisalabad, Hyderabad, Peshawar, Quetta, Jhang, Sargodha and Skardu. It is written, spoken and used in all provinces/territories of Pakistan although the people from differing provinces may have different indigenous languages, as from the fact that it is the "base language" of the country.
viki_text: Urdu is widely spoken in Pakistan and India, though it also has large number of speakers in the Middle East, United Kingdom, and North America.

wiki_text: A word that consists of a single syllable (like English dog) is called a monosyllable (and is said to be monosyllabic).
viki_text: Monosyllables have only one vowel sound; polysyllables have more than one.

wiki_text: English allows very complicated syllables; syllables may begin with up to three consonants (as in string or splash), and occasionally end with as many as four (as in prompts).
viki_text: Some languages like English have many kinds of closed syllables.

wiki_text: Many other languages are much more restricted; Japanese, for example, only allows and a chroneme in a coda, and theoretically has no consonant clusters at all, as the onset is composed of at most one consonant. Ambisyllabicity
viki_text: Some languages like Japanese have few kinds of closed syllables.

wiki_text: This syllable can be abstracted as a consonant-vowel-consonant syllable, abbreviated CVC. Languages vary greatly in the restrictions on the sounds making up the onset, nucleus and coda of a syllable, according to what is termed a language's phonotactics.
viki_text: Patterns of syllables can be shown with C and V (C for 'consonant', V for 'vowel').

wiki_text: The western lowland gorilla population in the wild is faced by a number of factors that threaten its extinction.
viki_text: Western lowland gorillas remain critically endangered.

wiki_text: They are often found on the ground and are made up of up to 30 gorillas. Western lowland gorillas live in the smallest family groups of all gorillas, with an average of four to eight members in each.

wiki_text: Western lowland gorillas primarily live in rain forests, swamp forest, brush, secondary vegetation, clearing and forest edges, abandoned farming fields and riverine forests.
viki_text: Western lowland gorillas dwell in the rainforests of equatorial Africa.

wiki_text: Most colonists still hoped for reconciliation with Great Britain, even after fighting began in the American Revolutionary War at Lexington and Concord in April 1775.
viki_text: Fighting broke out in 1775 at Lexington and Concord, marking the beginning of the American Revolutionary War.

wiki_text: On June 11, 1776, Congress appointed a "Committee of Five" to draft a declaration, consisting of John Adams of Massachusetts, Benjamin Franklin of Pennsylvania, Thomas Jefferson of Virginia, Robert R. Livingston of New York, and Roger Sherman of Connecticut.
viki_text: On June 11, 1776, a committee consisting of John Adams of Massachusetts, Benjamin Franklin of Pennsylvania, Thomas Jefferson of Virginia, Robert R. Livingston of New York, and Roger Sherman of Connecticut (known as the "Committee of Five"), was formed to draft a formal resolution declaring the colonists' intent.

wiki_text: The Declaration became official when Congress voted for it on July 4; signatures of the delegates were not needed to make it official. The handwritten copy of the Declaration of Independence that was signed by Congress is dated July 4, 1776.
viki_text: After its adoption by Congress on July 4, 1776, a handwritten draft of the Declaration of Independence was signed by the President of Congress, John Hancock, and the secretary to Congress, Charles Thomson.

wiki_text: The Declaration explained why the Thirteen Colonies at war with the Kingdom of Great Britain regarded themselves as thirteen independent sovereign states, no longer under British rule. With the Declaration, these new states took a collective first step toward forming the United States of America.
viki_text: The Declaration of Independence is a joint statement agreed by a group of people representing the interests of the various thirteen Colonies.

wiki_text: The committee presented this copy to the Congress on June 28, 1776.
viki_text: The committee presented this version to the Continental Congress on June 28, 1776.

wiki_text: The wording of the Declaration of Independence was approved on July 4, 1776 and sent to the printer for publication.
viki_text: The full Declaration was rewritten somewhat in general session prior to its adoption by the Continental Congress on July 4, 1776, at the Pennsylvania State House (now known as Independence Hall).

wiki_text: Thomas Paine's pamphlet Common Sense was published in January 1776, just as it became clear in the colonies that the king was not inclined to act as a conciliator. Paine had only recently arrived in the colonies from England, and he argued in favor of colonial independence, advocating republicanism as an alternative to monarchy and hereditary rule. Common Sense made a persuasive and impassioned case for independence, which had not yet been given serious intellectual consideration in the American colonies.
viki_text: On January 10, 1776, Thomas Paine published (anonymously) a pamphlet titled Common Sense in which he outlined major problems with the British form of government (a constitutional monarchy) and several reasons the American colonies should separate themselves from Great Britain.

wiki_text: What is certain is that the committee discussed the general outline which the document should follow and decided that Jefferson would write the first draft.
viki_text: The committee decided that Jefferson - its youngest member and the youngest signer of the final Declaration - would write the first draft of the resolution.

viki_text: Age caused the some of the paint to come off, or fade, and other artists have painted over some of Fra Angelico's work while trying to repair the paintings. The top part of the altarpiece is in the National Gallery, London.

wiki_text: According to Vasari, Fra Angelico initially received training as an illuminator, possibly working with his older brother Benedetto who was also a Dominican and an illuminator.
viki_text: Fra Benedetto painted illuminated manuscripts. Fra Angelico possibly learnt to paint manuscripts as well.

wiki_text: Among his other pupils were Zanobi Strozzi.
viki_text: One of his other pupils was called Zanobi Strozzi, but he did not become as famous as Benozzo.

wiki_text: Between 1418 and 1436 he was at the convent of Fiesole, where he also executed a number of frescoes for the church and the Altarpiece, which was deteriorated but has since been restored.
viki_text: Between 1418 and 1436, Fra Angelico painted an altarpiece for the church in Fiesole, which has been damaged.

wiki_text: Cosimo had a cell reserved for himself at the friary in order that he might retreat from the world.
viki_text: Cosimo took the largest of the friars' rooms, called cells, at the convent so that he could have a quiet place for religious thought.

wiki_text: From 1447 to 1449 Fra Angelico was back at the Vatican, designing the frescoes for the Niccoline Chapel for Nicholas V.
viki_text: From 1447 to 1449, Fra Angelico was back at the Vatican, where Pope Nicholas V had built a small chapel where he could pray privately.

wiki_text: He was known to contemporaries as Fra Giovanni da Fiesole (Brother John of Fiesole) and Fra Giovanni Angelico (Angelic Brother John).
viki_text: People who knew him when he was older called him Fra Giovanni da Fiesole (Brother John from Fiesole).

wiki_text: I, Giovanni, am the flower of Tuscany.|Translation of epitaph
viki_text: I am Brother John, from Florence, the flower of Tuscany.

wiki_text: In 1436, Fra Angelico was one of a number of the friars from Fiesole who moved to the newly built convent or friary of San Marco in Florence.
viki_text: In 1436, a new Dominican Convent of San Marco was established in Florence in some older monastery buildings. Fra Angelico was one of the friars from Fiesole who moved to Florence.

wiki_text: In 1445 Pope Eugene IV summoned him to Rome to paint the frescoes of the Chapel of the Holy Sacrament at St Peter's, later demolished by Pope Paul III.
viki_text: In 1445, Pope Eugenius IV sent a message for Fra Angelico to come to Rome to paint the frescoes of the Chapel of the Holy Sacrament at St Peter's. The chapel was later demolished by Pope Paul III to make way for the new St.Peter's Basilica and Fra Angelico's work was lost.

wiki_text: In 1447 Fra Angelico was in Orvieto with his pupil, Benozzo Gozzoli, executing works for the Cathedral.
viki_text: In 1447 Fra Angelico went to the city of Orvieto with his pupil, Benozzo Gozzoli, to paint some pictures for the Cathedral there .

wiki_text: In 1982, Pope John Paul II proclaimed his beatification in recognition of the holiness of his life, thereby making the title of "Blessed" official.
viki_text: The name "Blessed Angelico" has now been made official, because in 1982 Pope John Paul II "conferred beatification" which means that he is now on the way to being made a saint.

wiki_text: In modern Italian he is called Beato Angelico (Blessed Angelic One); the common English name Fra Angelico means the "Angelic friar".
viki_text: The Italians usually call him il Beato Angelico (the Blessed Angelico).

wiki_text: It shows Christ in Glory surrounded by more than 250 figures, including beatified Dominicans.
viki_text: It shows Christ in Glory, surrounded by more than 250 figures, and is a large and magnificent piece of work.

wiki_text: It was, according to Vasari, at Cosimo's urging that Fra Angelico set about the task of decorating the convent, including the magnificent fresco of the Chapter House, the often-reproduced Annunciation at the top of the stairs leading to the cells, the Maesta (or Coronation of the Madonna) with Saints (cell 9) and the many other devotional frescoes, of smaller format but remarkable luminous quality, depicting aspects of the Life of Christ that adorn the walls of each cell.
viki_text: Cosimo employed Fra Angelico to paint more pictures for the convent. All the cells for the friars were built upstairs around the sides of a square courtyard with a garden in the middle (called a cloister). On the wall at the top of the stairs, Fra Angelico painted a fresco of the Angel Gabriel announcing the birth of Jesus to the Blessed Virgin Mary.

wiki_text: The former Dominican convent of San Marco in Florence, now a state museum, holds several manuscripts that are thought to be entirely or partly by his hand.

wiki_text: The small chapel, with its brightly frescoed walls and gold leaf decorations gives the impression of a jewel box.

wiki_text: Vasari claims that at this time Fra Angelico was offered the Archbishopric of Florence by Pope Nicholas V, and that he refused it, recommending another friar for the position. The story seems possible and even likely.
viki_text: Vasari says that one of the popes (it might have been Eugenius or Pope Nichoas V) asked Fra Angelico if he would like the very important job of Archbishop of Florence. But Fra Angelico did not want such a high job and he suggested the name of another friar for the position.

wiki_text: Vasari wrote of Fra Angelico that "it is impossible to bestow too much praise on this holy father, who was so humble and modest in all that he did and said and whose pictures were painted with such facility and piety."
viki_text: Vasari says about him: "It is impossible to think of enough good things to say about this holy father, who was so humble and modest in everything that he did and said, and whose pictures were painted with such cleverness and holy faith."

wiki_text: The Bangladesh Rifles revolt was a mutiny staged on 25 and 26 February 2009 in Dhaka by a section of the Bangladesh Rifles (BDR), a paramilitary force mainly tasked with guarding the borders of Bangladesh.
viki_text: The Bangladesh Rifles Revolt ("BDR Mutiny") took place on 25 and 26 February 2009 in Peelkhana, Dhaka, organized by a group of the then BDR. After the mutiny, the name of the organization was changed to Border Guard Bangladesh or BGB for short. The company is now known as BGB. It is a paramilitary force whose main job is to guard the borders of Bangladesh.

wiki_text: The Director General of the BDR, Shakil Ahmed, was killed early during the first day of the revolt, along with dozens of other BDR senior commanders of the BDR, when rebels attacked the residences of the officers and killed Ahmed.

wiki_text: As long as species have been evolving, species have been going extinct.
viki_text: Even earlier types of humans have become extinct.

wiki_text: In ecology, extinction is often used informally to refer to local extinction, in which a species ceases to exist in the chosen area of study, but may still exist elsewhere. This phenomenon is also known as extirpation.
viki_text: Extinction is a term used by scientists to describe a situation where a particular organism, plant or animal, no longer exists on the Earth.

wiki_text: In the natural course of events, species become extinct for a number of reasons, including but not limited to: extinction of a necessary host, prey or pollinator, inter-species competition, inability to deal with evolving diseases and changing environmental conditions which can act to introduce novel predators, or to remove prey.
viki_text: There are lots of different reasons why creatures and plants become extinct.

wiki_text: A 2005 poll indicates that 86.3% use primarily Bokmål as their daily written language, 5.5% use both Bokmål and Nynorsk, and 7.5% use primarily Nynorsk.

wiki_text: A referendum (plural: referendums or less commonly referenda) is a direct and universal vote in which an entire electorate is invited to vote on a particular proposal, it can be nationwide or local forms.

wiki_text: In Switzerland, for example, multiple choice referendums are common.
viki_text: In Switzerland referendums are the most common.

wiki_text: In some countries, it is synonymous with a plebiscite or a vote on a ballot question.
viki_text: Some countries use the words plebiscite or ballot question, but it is basically the same.

wiki_text: This may result in the adoption of a new policy or specific law.

wiki_text: In Greek mythology, Medusa (; Μέδουσα "guardian, protectress") was a monster, a Gorgon, generally described as a winged human female with living venomous snakes in place of hair.
viki_text: Medusa is a character in Greekmythology.

wiki_text: In a late version of the Medusa myth, related by the Roman poet Ovid (Metamorphoses 4.770), Medusa was originally a ravishingly beautiful maiden, "the jealous aspiration of many suitors," but because Poseidon had raped her in Athena's temple, the enraged Athena transformed Medusa's beautiful hair to serpents and made her face so terrible to behold that the mere sight of it would turn onlookers to stone.
viki_text: She attracted the notice of Poseidon, the Sea God. The two had intimate relations in Temple of Athena. The goddess was outraged that her temple was used this way, and transformed Medusa into a monster with snakes for hair. Any man who looked upon her was turned to stone.

wiki_text: Although the mass media commonly reports earthquake magnitudes as "Richter magnitude" or "Richter scale", standard practice by most seismological authorities is to express an earthquake's strength on the moment magnitude scale, which is based on the actual energy released by an earthquake.
viki_text: The magnitude is the strength of the earthquake, which is the real energy measured by seismographs; is the unit of measurement used in the scale Richter , which is named after the scientist who designed in 1935..

wiki_text: An earthquake (also known as a quake, tremor or temblor) is the shaking of the surface of the Earth, resulting from the sudden release of energy in the Earth's lithosphere that creates seismic waves. Earthquakes can range in size from those that are so weak that they cannot be felt to those violent enough to toss people around and destroy whole cities. The seismicity, or seismic activity, of an area is the frequency, type and size of earthquakes experienced over a period of time.
viki_text: Earthquake The earthquakes or seism are sudden movements of the earth's surface that demonstrate the existence of huge internal forces at Earth.

wiki_text: Around halfway through his career, Edison attributed the hearing impairment to being struck on the ears by a train conductor when his chemical laboratory in a boxcar caught fire and he was thrown off the train in Smiths Creek, Michigan, along with his apparatus and chemicals. In his later years, he modified the story to say the injury occurred when the conductor, in helping him onto a moving train, lifted him by the ears. Being completely deaf in one ear and barely hearing in the other, Edison would listen to a music player or piano by chomping into the wood to absorb the sound waves into his skull.
viki_text: The effects of the fever, as well as getting picked up by the ears by a train conductor, caused Edison to become completely deaf in his left ear, and 80 percent deaf in the other.

wiki_text: A national anthem (also state anthem, national hymn, national song, etc.) is generally a patriotic musical composition that evokes and eulogizes the history, traditions, and struggles of its people, recognized either by a nation's government as the official national song, or by convention through use by the people.
viki_text: A National anthem is the national song or hymn of a country, used to officially recognise it.

wiki_text: There may also be royal anthems, presidential anthems, state anthems etc. for special occasions. They are played on national holidays and festivals, and have also come to be closely connected with sporting events.

wiki_text: Proposed following the often bitter 1787-88 debate over the ratification of the Constitution, and written to address the objections raised by Anti-Federalists, the Bill of Rights amendments add to the Constitution specific guarantees of personal freedoms and rights, clear limitations on the government's power in judicial and other proceedings, and explicit declarations that all powers not specifically granted to the U.S. Congress by the Constitution are reserved for the states or the people.
viki_text: The Bill of Rights is the name for the first ten amendments to the United States Constitution, which limit the power of the federal government and guarantee citizens of the United States certain rights.

wiki_text: Málaga (, ) is a municipality, capital of the Province of Málaga, in the Autonomous Community of Andalusia, Spain.

wiki_text: Málaga enjoys plenty of sunshine throughout the year, with an average of about 300 days of sunshine and only about 40-45 with precipitation annually.
viki_text: Málaga enjoys a Mediterranean climate, with over 2900 hours of sunshine per year.

wiki_text: Rain occurs mainly in winter, with summer being generally dry.
viki_text: Summers and dry and most rainfall occurs between November and March.

wiki_text: The painter and sculptor Pablo Picasso, Hebrew poet and Jewish philosopher Solomon Ibn Gabirol and the actor Antonio Banderas were born in Málaga.

wiki_text: In one version of this definition, winter begins at the winter solstice and ends at the vernal equinox.
viki_text: However, it officially begins on the winter solstice (mid December) and ends on the vernal equinox (mid March).

wiki_text: In some cultures, the season is regarded as beginning at the solstice and ending on the following equinox - in the Northern Hemisphere, depending on the year, this corresponds to the period between 20, 21 or 22 December and 19, 20 or 21 March.
viki_text: However, it officially begins on the winter solstice (mid June) and ends on the vernal equinox (mid September).

wiki_text: These dates are somewhat later than those used to define the beginning and end of the meteorological winter - usually considered to span the entirety of December, January, and February in the Northern Hemisphere and June, July, and August in the Southern.
viki_text: June, July, and August are generally considered the months of winter in the Southern Hemisphere.

wiki_text: Winter is often defined by meteorologists to be the three calendar months with the lowest average temperatures. This corresponds to the months of December, January and February in the Northern Hemisphere, and June, July and August in the Southern Hemisphere.
viki_text: December, January, and February are generally considered the months of winter in the Northern Hemisphere.

wiki_text: It has a longer snout than other sturgeons and has four barbels at the side of its mouth. Lifecycle Atlantic sturgeon under six years of age stay in the brackish water where they were born before moving into the ocean.
viki_text: Atlantic sturgeon may live 30-60 years.

wiki_text: The Atlantic sturgeon (Acipenser oxyrinchus oxyrinchus) is a member of the family Acipenseridae and along with other sturgeon it is sometimes considered a living fossil.

wiki_text: In captivity, zoos typically maintain the giant panda's bamboo diet, though some will provide specially formulated biscuits or other dietary supplements. Pandas will travel between different habitats if they need to, so they can get the nutrients that they need and to balance their diet for reproduction.
viki_text: In zoos, pandas have a more varied diet.

wiki_text: The giant panda has luxuriant black-and-white fur.
viki_text: The giant panda is about the size of an American black bear.

wiki_text: The giant panda is among the world's most adored and protected rare animals, and is one of the few in the world whose natural inhabitant status was able to gain a UNESCO World Heritage Site designation.
viki_text: The giant panda is one of the most endangered species on earth.

wiki_text: The giant panda lives in a few mountain ranges in central China, mainly in Sichuan, but also in neighbouring Shaanxi and Gansu.
viki_text: It lives in the mountain forests of central China.

wiki_text: The giant panda's paw has a "thumb" and five fingers; the "thumb" - actually a modified sesamoid bone - helps it to hold bamboo while eating.
viki_text: Giant pandas have five fingers and a "thumb" on their paws.

wiki_text: The mating season is between March and May, when a female goes into estrus, which lasts for two or three days and only occurs once a year.

wiki_text: The Confederate States Army was the military land force of the Confederate States of America (commonly referred to as the Confederacy) during the American Civil War (1861-1865), fighting against the United States forces.

wiki_text: After the social construct of adolescence was created, adulthood split into two forms: biological adulthood and social adulthood. Thus, there are now two primary forms of adults: biological adults (people who have attained reproductive ability, are fertile, or who evidence secondary sex characteristics) and social adults (people who are recognized by their culture or law as being adults).
viki_text: In biology, people are adults when they have gone through puberty, and can make children.

wiki_text: In human context, the term adult additionally has meanings associated with social and legal concepts. In contrast to a "minor", a legal adult is a person who has attained the age of majority and is therefore regarded as independent, self-sufficient, and .
viki_text: An adult is a human term, that means a fully-grown human: one that isn't a child anymore.

wiki_text: The age of majority ranges internationally from ages 15 to 21, with 18 being the most common age.
viki_text: This age is often 18, but it can vary depending on the different countries.

wiki_text: Demographics With an estimated 2017 population of 67.15 million people, France is the 20th most populous country in the world, the third-most populous in Europe (after Russia and Germany), and the second most populous in the European Union (after Germany). France is an outlier among developed countries in general, and European countries in particular, in having a relatively high rate of natural population growth: by birth rates alone, it was responsible for almost all natural population growth in the European Union in 2006.
viki_text: More than 66 million inhabitants live in France, making it the most populous country of the European Union with high demographic growth.

wiki_text: France receives the highest number of tourists per year, largely thanks to the numerous cultural establishments and historical buildings implanted all over the territory.
viki_text: France is the first country in the world in terms of tourism: its landscapes are varied, there are castles, seaside resorts, ski resorts, mountainous areas and even extinct volcanos.

wiki_text: The UNESCO inscribed 41 sites in France on the World Heritage List.
viki_text: It has 38 sites designated by UNESCO (ranked 4th in the world for most UNESCO sites) and has many places among the most visited in the world: the Eiffel Tower, the Louvre, Mont St Michel.

wiki_text: After around 100 - 300 million years, the first stars formed, known as Population III stars.

wiki_text: In the earliest stages of the universe, tiny fluctuations within the universe's density led to concentrations of dark matter gradually forming. Ordinary matter, attracted to these by gravity, formed large gas clouds and eventually, stars and galaxies, where the dark matter was most dense, and voids where it was least dense.
viki_text: Tiny fluctuations in the density of matter early on in the Universe acted like seeds around which matter began to clump, setting the scene for the formation of vast clusters of galaxies.

wiki_text: The Universe is often defined as "the totality of existence", or everything that exists, everything that has existed, and everything that will exist. The word universe may also refer to concepts such as the cosmos, the world, and nature. Etymology
viki_text: The Universe is the name astronomers give to the vast space which contains everything we know, from great clusters of galaxies to the tiniest of particles.

wiki_text: Ebola virus disease (EVD), also known as Ebola hemorrhagic fever (EHF) or simply Ebola, is a viral hemorrhagic fever of humans and other primates caused by ebolaviruses. The disease has a high risk of death, killing 25% to 90% of those infected, with an average of about 50%.
viki_text: Medical waste from Ebola patients is burnt The Ebola virus disease is a dangerous virus disease that can kill humans and other primates such as gorillas.

wiki_text: As earlier with Perugino and others, Raphael was able to assimilate the influence of Florentine art, whilst keeping his own developing style.

wiki_text: In the very small court of Urbino he was probably more integrated into the central circle of the ruling family than most court painters.

wiki_text: Most modern historians agree that Raphael at least worked as an assistant to Perugino from around 1500; the influence of Perugino on Raphael's early work is very clear: "probably no other pupil of genius has ever absorbed so much of his master's teaching as Raphael did", according to Wölfflin.
viki_text: Raphael was about 15 when he joined the workshop of Pietro Perugino, one of the famous artists of the period.

wiki_text: In Christian theology, the Immaculate Conception is the conception of the Virgin Mary free from original sin by virtue of the merits of her son Jesus.
viki_text: The Immaculate Conception is the catholic belief that the Virgin Mary did not inherit the original sin that is in any other human being since Adam and Eve ate the Forbidden Fruit in Paradise.

wiki_text: The doctrine of the Immaculate Conception (Mary being conceived free from original sin) is not to be confused with the virginal conception of her son Jesus.
viki_text: This belief is different from the virginal conception of Jesus, the belief that Mary became pregnant without sexual intercourse.

wiki_text: As the Nile was such an important factor in Egyptian life, the ancient calendar was even based on the three cycles of the Nile.
viki_text: The ancient Egyptian calendar was based on the 3 cycles of the Nile.

wiki_text: Because the river overflowed its banks annually and deposited new layers of silt, the surrounding land was very fertile.
viki_text: Silt deposits from the Nile made the surrounding land fertile because the river overflowed its banks annually.

wiki_text: Below Renk the White Nile enters Sudan, it flows north to Khartoum and meets the Blue Nile.
viki_text: The White Nile flows from Lake Victoria in Uganda, and through Sudan to Khartoum.

wiki_text: During the dry season the natural discharge of the Blue Nile can be as low as , although upstream dams regulate the flow of the river. During the wet season the peak flow of the Blue Nile often exceeds in late August (a difference of a factor of 50).
viki_text: During the dry season the natural discharge of the Blue Nile can be as low as 113 m3/s in late August (a difference of a factor of 50).

wiki_text: Peret was the growing season, and Shemu, the last season, was the harvest season when there were no rains.

wiki_text: Role in the founding of Egyptian civilization The Greek historian Herodotus wrote that "Egypt was the gift of the Nile". An unending source of sustenance, it played a crucial role in the development of Egyptian civilization.
viki_text: The Nile provided a great role in the development of Egyptian civilization.

wiki_text: The Blue Nile (, ʿĀbay) springs from Lake Tana in the Ethiopian Highlands.
viki_text: The Blue Nile springs from Lake Tana in the Ethiopian Highlands, under tropical climate with dry and wet seasons.

wiki_text: The Blue Nile flows about 1,400 kilometres to Khartoum, where the Blue Nile and White Nile join to form the Nile.
viki_text: The Blue Nile flows about 1,400 kilometres to Khartoum.

wiki_text: The White Nile is considered to be the headwaters and primary stream of the Nile itself.
viki_text: The Nile is one of the major rivers of the world.

wiki_text: The flow of the Blue Nile varies considerably over its yearly cycle and is the main contribution to the large natural variation of the Nile flow.
viki_text: The flow of the Blue Nile varies considerably over its yearly cycle and is the main contribution to the large natural variation of the Nile flow in Sudan and Egypt.

wiki_text: The northern section of the river flows north almost entirely through the Sudanese desert to Egypt, then ends in a large delta and flows into the Mediterranean Sea.
viki_text: It begins near the equator and ends in a large delta into the Mediterranean Sea near Alexandria, Egypt.

wiki_text: Anna Mary Robertson Moses (September 7, 1860 - December 13, 1961), known by her nickname Grandma Moses, was an American folk artist.
viki_text: Anna Mary Robertson Moses (better known by her nickname of "Grandma Moses") was an American folk painter. She was born on September 7, 1860 in Greenwich, New York, and died on December 13, 1961.

wiki_text: Moses painted scenes of rural life
viki_text: Moses painted mostly scenes of rural life and activities such as maple sugaring and ice skating parties.

wiki_text: She began painting in earnest at the age of 78 and is often cited as an example of an individual who successfully began a career in the arts at an advanced age. Her works have been shown and sold in the United States and abroad and have been marketed on greeting cards and other merchandise. Moses' paintings are displayed in the collections of many museums.
viki_text: Moses began her career in painting in her 70s.

wiki_text: She expressed an interest in art throughout her life, including embroidery of pictures with yarn, until arthritis made this pursuit too painful.
viki_text: For years she had embroidered pictures, but arthritis eventually made this work impossible.

wiki_text: After uptake in human cells, iron storage is precisely regulated.

wiki_text: Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional deficiency in the world. When loss of iron is not adequately compensated by adequate dietary iron intake, a state of latent iron deficiency occurs, which over time leads to iron-deficiency anemia if left untreated, which is characterised by an insufficient number of red blood cells and an insufficient amount of hemoglobin.
viki_text: Not enough iron in the body is called anemia.

wiki_text: In tropical climates there are often only two seasons: a wet season and a dry season.
viki_text: The seasonal variations are dominated by the difference of precipitation : there are a wet season and a dry season.

wiki_text: In tropical climates, the temperature remains relatively constant (hot) throughout the year.
viki_text: In this climate all twelve months have mean temperatures of at least 18 °C/64 °F relatively constant throughout the year.

wiki_text: In tropical rain forest climates, there is either no dry season or at most two dry months.
viki_text: These climates generally have a pronounced dry season (seven or more dry season months) with the driest month having precipitation less than 60 mm and also less than .

wiki_text: This type of climate results from the monsoon winds which change direction according to the seasons.
viki_text: This type of climate, most common in India , South and Central America, results from the monsoon winds which change direction according to the seasons.

wiki_text: As in the First World War, Sweden remained officially neutral during World War II, although its neutrality during World War II has been disputed.

wiki_text: At , Sweden is the 55th-largest country in the world, the 4th-largest country entirely in Europe, and the largest in Northern Europe.
viki_text: Sweden is the third largest country by area in Northern Europe and fourth in all of Europe.

wiki_text: Because of Sweden's high latitude, the length of daylight varies greatly. North of the Arctic Circle, the sun never sets for part of each summer, and it never rises for part of each winter.
viki_text: In the summer the sun hardly sets in Sweden, especially in the far north where it does not set at all.

wiki_text: However, Sweden is much warmer and drier than other places at a similar latitude, and even somewhat farther south, mainly because of the combination of the Gulf Stream and the general west wind drift, caused by the direction of planet Earth's rotation.
viki_text: Sweden is much warmer than other places in the same latitude, mainly because of the Gulf Stream.

wiki_text: Most Swedish immigrants moved to the Midwestern United States, with a large population in Minnesota, with a few others moving to other parts of the United States and Canada. wiki_text: A distinctive feature of a violin body is its hourglass-like shape and the arching of its top and back.
